_meta.description: Hubble's Law end-of-activity question bank. Two question types: 'data-click' (student clicks a cell in the observation log table) and 'conceptual' (multiple choice). Galaxy indices: 0=Alpha, 1=Beta, 2=Gamma, 3=Delta, 4=Epsilon. Column keys match table: name, period, dist, wave, z, vel.

questions.question: Click on the NAME of the galaxy that is farthest from Earth.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon has the greatest distance (90 Mpc), making it the farthest galaxy in your data.

questions.question: Click on the DISTANCE value of the galaxy closest to Earth.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha is only about 10 Mpc away — the closest galaxy you observed.

questions.question: Click on the VELOCITY of the fastest-receding galaxy in your data.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon has the highest recession velocity at approximately 6,300 km/s.

questions.question: Click on the REDSHIFT (z) value of Galaxy Alpha.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha has the smallest redshift, around z ≈ 0.0023, because it is the closest galaxy.

questions.question: Click on the PERIOD of the Cepheid with the longest pulsation cycle.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon's Cepheid has a period of about 30 days — the longest in your data, meaning it is the most luminous.

questions.question: Click on the OBSERVED WAVELENGTH (λ_obs) that is most shifted from the reference wavelength of 400 nm.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon's absorption line is at ~408.4 nm — shifted the most from the 400 nm reference, indicating the greatest redshift.

questions.question: Click on the NAME of the galaxy whose Cepheid has the shortest pulsation period.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha's Cepheid has a period of about 10 days — the shortest and therefore least luminous Cepheid in the set.

questions.question: Click on the VELOCITY of the slowest-moving galaxy.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha has the lowest recession velocity (~700 km/s) because it is the closest galaxy.

questions.question: Click on the DISTANCE of Galaxy Gamma.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Gamma is approximately 40 Mpc from Earth.

questions.question: Click on the REDSHIFT value of the galaxy with the greatest redshift.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon has the greatest redshift (z ≈ 0.021) because it is the most distant and fastest-receding galaxy.

questions.question: Click on the PERIOD of Galaxy Delta's Cepheid variable.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Delta's Cepheid has a period of about 12 days.

questions.question: Click on the VELOCITY of Galaxy Beta.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Beta has a recession velocity of approximately 1,750 km/s.

questions.question: Click on the OBSERVED WAVELENGTH of the galaxy nearest to Earth.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha is closest and has the smallest wavelength shift (~400.93 nm), barely shifted from 400 nm.

questions.question: Click on the DISTANCE of the galaxy with the highest velocity.
questions.explanation: The galaxy with the highest velocity (Epsilon) is also the farthest away (~90 Mpc). This is exactly what Hubble's Law predicts!

questions.question: Click on the NAME of the galaxy with the second-shortest Cepheid period.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Delta's Cepheid has a period of about 12 days — the second shortest after Alpha's 10 days.

questions.question: Click on the REDSHIFT of the galaxy that is approximately 40 Mpc away.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Gamma is at about 40 Mpc and has a redshift of z ≈ 0.0093.

questions.question: Click on the VELOCITY of the galaxy whose Cepheid has the longest period.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Epsilon has the longest-period Cepheid (~30 days) and a velocity of ~6,300 km/s.

questions.question: Click on the PERIOD value that corresponds to the MOST luminous Cepheid in your data.
questions.explanation: Since luminosity = period × 1000, the longest period (Epsilon, ~30 days) means the highest luminosity (L ≈ 30,000).

questions.question: Click on the DISTANCE of Galaxy Beta.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Beta is approximately 25 Mpc from Earth.

questions.question: Click on the WAVELENGTH of the galaxy moving at approximately 4,200 km/s.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Delta has a velocity of ~4,200 km/s and an observed wavelength of ~405.6 nm.

questions.question: Click on the NAME of the galaxy with the LEAST redshift.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Alpha has the smallest redshift because it is closest to Earth, so its light is barely stretched.

questions.question: Click on the VELOCITY of the galaxy at approximately 60 Mpc from Earth.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Delta is at ~60 Mpc and has a recession velocity of approximately 4,200 km/s.

questions.question: Click on the PERIOD of the Cepheid in a galaxy at approximately 25 Mpc.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Beta is at ~25 Mpc, and its Cepheid has a period of about 16 days.

questions.question: Click on the NAME of the galaxy whose Cepheid period is closest to 25 days.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Gamma's Cepheid has a period of approximately 25 days.

questions.question: Click on the REDSHIFT of Galaxy Delta.
questions.explanation: Galaxy Delta has a redshift of approximately z ≈ 0.014.

questions.question: What does it mean when a galaxy's spectrum is redshifted?
choices: The galaxy is moving away from us
The galaxy is moving toward us
questions.explanation: Redshift means the light waves are being stretched to longer (redder) wavelengths, which happens when a galaxy is moving away from us — like a cosmic Doppler effect.

questions.question: According to Hubble's Law, what happens to a galaxy's recession velocity as its distance from us increases?
questions.explanation: Hubble's Law (v = H₀ × D) says velocity is directly proportional to distance — farther galaxies move away faster.

questions.question: Why are Cepheid variable stars useful for measuring distances to other galaxies?
choices: They are the closest stars to Earth
Their pulsation period reveals their true brightness, allowing distance calculation
They are the hottest stars in the universe
questions.explanation: Cepheids are 'standard candles' — by timing their pulse, we know their true luminosity. Comparing that to how dim they appear tells us the distance.

questions.question: What relationship did Henrietta Swan Leavitt discover about Cepheid variables?
choices: Brighter stars are always closer to Earth
Longer-period Cepheids have greater intrinsic luminosity
All stars pulse at the same rate
questions.explanation: Leavitt's Law: the longer a Cepheid takes to complete one full brightness cycle, the more luminous it truly is.

questions.question: If a galaxy has a very large redshift, what can you conclude about its distance?
choices: It is relatively close to Earth
It is relatively far from Earth
questions.explanation: Hubble's Law tells us that larger redshift means greater velocity, which corresponds to greater distance.

questions.question: What does the Hubble Constant (H₀) represent?
choices: The rate at which the universe is expanding
questions.explanation: H₀ tells us how fast the universe is expanding — specifically, how much faster a galaxy recedes for each additional Megaparsec of distance.

questions.question: Why do astronomers call Cepheid variable stars 'standard candles'?
choices: Their known brightness makes them useful for measuring cosmic distances
They give off heat like a candle flame
They are all exactly the same size
questions.explanation: Just like knowing how bright a candle is helps you judge how far away it is, knowing a Cepheid's true luminosity lets astronomers calculate its distance.

questions.question: If you observe a Cepheid with a very long period, what do you know about its intrinsic luminosity?
choices: It is very close to Earth
questions.explanation: Leavitt's Law: longer period = greater intrinsic luminosity. In this activity, L = Period × 1000.

questions.question: What does the slope of your Hubble's Law graph (Velocity vs. Distance) represent?
questions.explanation: Since v = H₀ × D, the slope of the velocity-vs-distance line is the Hubble Constant.

questions.question: What is the reference wavelength (λ₀) used for hydrogen absorption in this experiment?
questions.explanation: The lab reference wavelength is 400 nm. When we observe a galaxy, that line shifts to a longer wavelength due to the galaxy's motion.

questions.question: What causes absorption lines in a galaxy's spectrum to shift to longer wavelengths?
choices: The galaxy is moving toward us
The galaxy is moving away from us
The galaxy is rotating on its axis
The galaxy's stars are getting cooler
questions.explanation: When a galaxy moves away, its light waves get stretched — shifting absorption lines toward the red (longer wavelength) end of the spectrum.

questions.question: How can the inverse of the Hubble Constant (1/H₀) be used?
choices: It gives the speed of light
It provides a rough estimate of the age of the universe
It measures the temperature of stars
It calculates the size of individual galaxies
questions.explanation: Since v = H₀ × D and v = D/t, the time t ≈ 1/H₀ gives us a rough age for the universe. This is sometimes called the 'Hubble Time'.

questions.question: If two Cepheids have the same intrinsic luminosity but one appears much dimmer through the telescope, what can you conclude?
choices: The dimmer one is farther away
They are at the same distance
questions.explanation: Light gets dimmer with distance (the inverse square law). If two stars have the same true brightness but one looks fainter, it must be farther away.

questions.question: What is the correct formula for redshift (z) used in this activity?
questions.explanation: Redshift is the fractional change in wavelength: z = (observed − reference) / reference = (λ_obs − 400) / 400.

questions.question: What does the Big Bang Theory predict about the motion of distant galaxies?
choices: They should all be moving toward us
They should be moving away, with more distant ones moving faster
They should be orbiting our galaxy
questions.explanation: The Big Bang Theory predicts an expanding universe where space itself is stretching — so more distant galaxies are carried away faster, exactly what your data shows!

questions.question: What would a BLUESHIFT in a galaxy's spectrum indicate?
choices: The galaxy is moving away from us
The galaxy is moving toward us
The galaxy is expanding in size
questions.explanation: Blueshift (wavelengths compressed shorter/bluer) means the source is approaching. The Andromeda galaxy is actually blueshifted — it's heading toward the Milky Way!

questions.question: The speed of light used in the velocity calculation (v = z × c) is approximately:
questions.explanation: The speed of light (c) is approximately 300,000 km/s or 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

questions.question: If the universe is expanding, what was it like in the distant past?
choices: The same size as it is now
questions.explanation: Running the expansion backwards in time means everything was once packed much closer together — the early universe was extremely small, dense, and hot.

questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest evidence from YOUR data that supports the expanding universe?
choices: All galaxies have exactly the same velocity
More distant galaxies have greater redshifts and higher velocities
questions.explanation: Your data shows a clear pattern: as distance increases, so do redshift and velocity. This is Hubble's Law in action — direct evidence for an expanding universe.

questions.question: What unit is distance measured in for this activity?
questions.explanation: One Megaparsec (Mpc) equals about 3.26 million light-years. Astronomers use Mpc because individual light-years would be inconveniently huge numbers at galactic distances.

questions.question: A Cepheid variable star physically expands and contracts. What effect does this have on its light?
choices: It pulses brighter and dimmer in a regular cycle
It gets dimmer over time and never recovers
It emits X-rays only when expanding
questions.explanation: As a Cepheid expands it brightens, and as it contracts it dims — creating a repeating cycle of brightness that astronomers can time precisely.

questions.question: Why is it important that redshift increases proportionally with distance?
choices: It proves stars are made of hydrogen
It shows the universe is shrinking
It supports the idea of a uniform expansion of space — consistent with the Big Bang
It means all galaxies formed at the same time
questions.explanation: A proportional relationship (Hubble's Law) means space is expanding uniformly in all directions — exactly what we'd expect if everything originated from a single point (the Big Bang).

questions.question: In the formula D = √(L/B), what does 'B' represent?
choices: The star's apparent brightness as measured by our sensor
questions.explanation: B is the apparent (observed) brightness — how bright the star looks from Earth. Comparing this to L (true luminosity) lets us solve for distance.

questions.question: If every galaxy you observed showed zero redshift (z = 0), what would that suggest about the universe?
choices: The universe is static — not expanding or contracting
questions.explanation: Zero redshift means no wavelength shift, which means galaxies aren't moving away (or toward us). This would imply a static, non-expanding universe.

questions.question: Edwin Hubble's discovery that galaxies are moving away from us was revolutionary because it showed that:
choices: The Earth is at the exact center of the universe
Stars can only exist inside galaxies
The universe itself is expanding and had a beginning
Gravity does not work at large distances
questions.explanation: Hubble's observations proved the universe is not eternal and static — it is expanding, which means it must have been smaller in the past. This was the first observational evidence for what we now call the Big Bang.
